content: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is the most common endocrine disorder in reproductive-age women, affecting 6-12% prevalence. Characterized by hyperandrogenism (clinical: hirsutism, acne, alopecia; or biochemical: elevated testosterone), ovulatory dysfunction (oligo/anovulation manifesting as irregular menses), and polycystic ovarian morphology on ultrasound. Pathophysiology involves insulin resistance (present in ~70% regardless of BMI), hyperinsulinemia stimulating ovarian androgen production, altered gonadotropin secretion (elevated LH:FSH ratio), and genetic predisposition. Rotterdam criteria (2003) require ≥2 of 3 features for diagnosis. Long-term health risks include type 2 diabetes (4-fold increased risk), metabolic syndrome, cardiovascular disease, endometrial cancer (from unopposed estrogen), infertility, mood disorders.
